items.prompt_md: In the streaming k-th-largest heap, why use a **min-heap** even though you want the largest values?
options: Because heapq only supports min-heaps, so it's forced
Because the root of the size-k min-heap is the smallest of the top k — exactly the k-th largest value
Because a min-heap pops in O(1) while a max-heap pops in O(log k)
Because min-heaps use less memory than max-heaps
items.answer: Because the root of the size-k min-heap is the smallest of the top k — exactly the k-th largest value
items.explanation_md: Capping a min-heap at size k means the k largest values seen so far all live in the heap, and the smallest of *those* — the root — is precisely the k-th largest overall.

items.prompt_md: What does the root of a size-k min-heap that holds the top-k-largest values seen so far represent?
options: The largest value overall
The k-th largest value overall — the weakest member currently inside the top k
The average of the top k values
An arbitrary value with no special meaning
items.answer: The k-th largest value overall — the weakest member currently inside the top k
items.explanation_md: Since the heap holds exactly the current top k, its smallest element is the boundary value: the k-th largest seen so far.

items.prompt_md: To adapt this size-k heap pattern to track the k **smallest** values streaming in instead of the k largest, what changes?
options: Nothing — the min-heap approach works unchanged
Negate incoming values (or use a max-heap) capped at size k, evicting the largest survivor when it overflows
Switch to a hash map keyed by value
Sort the whole stream once at the very end
items.answer: Negate incoming values (or use a max-heap) capped at size k, evicting the largest survivor when it overflows
items.explanation_md: For k smallest, you want to evict the current *largest* survivor when the heap overflows, so the heap must behave like a max-heap — negation flips heapq's min-heap into that role.

items.prompt_md: A new value arrives that is smaller than the current root of a size-k min-heap tracking the top-k largest. Following the standard push-then-trim code, what happens to it?
options: It gets pushed onto the heap and then immediately popped back off, since it isn't among the current top k
It is silently discarded without ever touching the heap, for efficiency
It directly overwrites the root without any heap operation
It triggers a full re-heapify of the entire structure
items.answer: It gets pushed onto the heap and then immediately popped back off, since it isn't among the current top k
items.explanation_md: Pushing it makes it the new minimum; since the heap now exceeds size k, popping removes exactly that value again — net effect: no change to the top-k set.

items.prompt_md: A candidate implementation keeps a heap of ALL seen values (never capped at k) and pops k times whenever it needs the k-th largest. What's the downside versus the size-k min-heap approach?
options: It's asymptotically identical, just implemented differently
The heap grows to size n, so each push costs O(log n) instead of O(log k), and each query costs O(k log n) instead of O(1)
It doesn't work at all for finding the k-th largest
It requires negation while the min-heap approach doesn't
items.answer: The heap grows to size n, so each push costs O(log n) instead of O(log k), and each query costs O(k log n) instead of O(1)
items.explanation_md: Without bounding the heap to size k, both insertion cost and query cost grow with the full stream length n instead of staying pinned to k.

items.prompt_md: Why does the constructor call `heapq.heapify(self.heap)` before trimming down to size k, rather than popping one at a time from an unsorted list?
options: heapify arranges the list into a valid heap in O(n), so subsequent heappop calls are correct and efficient
heapify fully sorts the list so heappop always returns index 0
heapify is only required when k equals n
heapify has no real effect; it's included only for readability
items.answer: heapify arranges the list into a valid heap in O(n), so subsequent heappop calls are correct and efficient
items.explanation_md: You cannot correctly `heappop` from an arbitrary list — it must satisfy the heap invariant first, which `heapify` establishes in linear time.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `heapq.heapify` on a list of n elements?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Building a heap bottom-up from an existing list takes linear time, not the O(n log n) you'd get from inserting n elements one at a time.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of each `add(val)` call once the heap is capped at size k?
options: O(log k)
O(k)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(log k)
items.explanation_md: Each push/pop touches a heap that never exceeds size k, so the cost is O(log k) regardless of how many values the stream has produced overall.

items.prompt_md: A stream delivers n values one at a time, and the k-th largest must be reported after each one. Compare the size-k heap approach to a naive re-sort after every value.
options: Both are O(n log n) — heaps don't help here
Heap: O(n log k); naive re-sort per value: O(n^2 log n)
Heap: O(n log n); naive re-sort per value: O(n log k)
Heap: O(n); naive re-sort per value: O(n log n)
items.answer: Heap: O(n log k); naive re-sort per value: O(n^2 log n)
items.explanation_md: The heap does O(log k) work per value for O(n log k) total. Re-sorting a growing list of up to n elements after every one of n insertions costs roughly O(n^2 log n).

items.prompt_md: Why is bounding the heap to size k important, rather than letting it grow to hold every value ever seen?
options: It isn't important — an unbounded heap gives the same asymptotic behavior
It keeps every push/pop at O(log k) instead of O(log n), and keeps memory at O(k) instead of O(n)
It's required because heapq physically cannot hold more than k elements
It avoids needing an initial heapify
items.answer: It keeps every push/pop at O(log k) instead of O(log n), and keeps memory at O(k) instead of O(n)
items.explanation_md: The entire point of the size-k cap is to decouple per-operation cost and memory from the total stream length n.

items.prompt_md: Which of these is a common bug when implementing 'top-k with a heap' for the k largest values?
options: Using a min-heap and capping it at size k
Using a max-heap (or negation) but forgetting to cap the size at k, so the root no longer reflects the k-th largest boundary
Popping the root once the heap exceeds size k
Reading `heap[0]` to get the k-th largest
items.answer: Using a max-heap (or negation) but forgetting to cap the size at k, so the root no longer reflects the k-th largest boundary
items.explanation_md: Getting the polarity right (min-heap for k largest) only works if the size is also bounded to k — an uncapped heap answers a different question entirely.

items.prompt_md: Given all n values up front (not streaming), what is the complexity of building a size-k top-k heap via heapify-then-trim (heapify, then pop until size k)?
options: O(n)
O(n log k)
O(n + (n-k) log n)
O(k)
items.answer: O(n + (n-k) log n)
items.explanation_md: Heapify costs O(n); trimming from n down to k pops (n-k) times, each an O(log n) operation on the shrinking heap.

items.prompt_md: A live leaderboard must show the current 3rd-highest score as new scores keep arriving one at a time. Which approach fits?
options: Re-sort all scores seen so far after every new score
Maintain a size-3 min-heap of the best scores; the root is always the 3rd-highest
Store scores in a hash map keyed by player name
Use two pointers over a sorted list of scores
items.answer: Maintain a size-3 min-heap of the best scores; the root is always the 3rd-highest
items.explanation_md: A running 'k-th highest under a live stream' query is the fingerprint for a size-k heap, not a full re-sort each time.

items.prompt_md: You must return the 5 largest values from a huge unsorted array, and their internal order among themselves doesn't matter.
options: Fully sort the array and take the last 5 elements
Maintain a size-5 min-heap while scanning once, keeping only the current top 5
Build a hash map of value counts
Use two pointers from both ends of the array
items.answer: Maintain a size-5 min-heap while scanning once, keeping only the current top 5
items.explanation_md: "k largest, k much smaller than n" is the trigger for a bounded size-k heap — O(n log k) beats a full O(n log n) sort.

items.prompt_md: Sensor readings stream in continuously, and at any moment you must report the current 10 smallest readings seen so far.
options: Re-sort the full history of readings every time you're asked
Maintain a size-10 max-heap (via negation), evicting the current largest survivor when it grows past 10
Use a stack to track readings in arrival order
Use BFS over the readings
items.answer: Maintain a size-10 max-heap (via negation), evicting the current largest survivor when it grows past 10
items.explanation_md: Streaming plus a running 'smallest k so far' query is the same top-k pattern, just with the heap polarity flipped for smallest instead of largest.

items.prompt_md: New elements keep getting added to a running collection, and after each addition you need the current k-th largest element without recomputing from scratch.
options: Recompute the k-th largest with a full sort after every addition
Maintain a size-k min-heap and read its root after each insertion
Track only the running sum and average
Use a hash set to deduplicate elements
items.answer: Maintain a size-k min-heap and read its root after each insertion
items.explanation_md: This is exactly the streaming k-th-largest setup: a size-k min-heap answers the query in O(log k) per insertion.

items.prompt_md: You need the top 100 highest-paid employees out of 10 million employee records, where 10 million is far larger than 100.
options: Sort all 10 million records by salary, then take the top 100
Maintain a size-100 min-heap while scanning once, keeping only the current top 100
Store salaries in a hash map keyed by employee id
Use two pointers from both ends of an unsorted list
items.answer: Maintain a size-100 min-heap while scanning once, keeping only the current top 100
items.explanation_md: With k this much smaller than n, a size-k heap's O(n log k) beats a full O(n log n) sort of every record.

items.prompt_md: A queue of incoming bids arrives one at a time; you must always be able to answer 'what is the 20th-highest bid so far?'
options: Store all bids in a list and sort on every query
Keep a size-20 min-heap of the highest bids so far; its root answers the query
Keep a hash map counting how many times each bid amount appears
Use a two-pointer scan over all bids each time
items.answer: Keep a size-20 min-heap of the highest bids so far; its root answers the query
items.explanation_md: A live 'k-th highest so far' query under a stream of arrivals is the size-k min-heap pattern.

items.prompt_md: You're asked for the k largest elements of a list, where k is a small fixed constant while n (the list length) can be arbitrarily large.
options: Sort the whole list — the constant factor doesn't matter
Maintain a size-k min-heap in one pass so total cost is O(n log k) instead of O(n log n)
Build a hash map of frequencies and read off the top entries
Use two pointers converging from each end
items.answer: Maintain a size-k min-heap in one pass so total cost is O(n log k) instead of O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Whenever k is fixed and small relative to n, the size-k heap's O(n log k) is asymptotically better than sorting everything.

items.prompt_md: Every time a new exam score is recorded, the teacher wants to know instantly if it's now among the class's top 3, without rechecking every past score.
options: Recheck every past score from scratch each time
Maintain a size-3 min-heap of the current top 3; compare new scores against its root
Track only the single maximum score
Use a stack of scores in the order they were recorded
items.answer: Maintain a size-3 min-heap of the current top 3; compare new scores against its root
items.explanation_md: A live 'am I in the current top k' check is answered in O(1) by comparing against a size-k min-heap's root.

items.prompt_md: A monitoring system stores millions of latency measurements and periodically needs just the worst (highest) 15 latencies, discarding the rest.
options: Keep every measurement sorted at all times
Maintain a size-15 min-heap that only keeps the current worst 15 latencies
Store latencies in a hash map keyed by timestamp
Use two pointers over the sorted measurements
items.answer: Maintain a size-15 min-heap that only keeps the current worst 15 latencies
items.explanation_md: Needing only a bounded top-k subset out of a huge stream, discarding everything else, is the size-k heap's exact use case.

items.prompt_md: You need the exact number of times each value appears in an array — not a ranking, just the raw counts.
options: Maintain a size-k min-heap of the most frequent values
Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each value in one pass
Sort the array and take the largest k values
Use two pointers from both ends of the array
items.answer: Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each value in one pass
items.explanation_md: There's no top-k or streaming-best question here, just exact counts — a hash map does this directly with no heap needed.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, find two numbers whose sum equals a target value.
options: Maintain a min-heap of the array values
Use two pointers starting at each end, moving inward based on the current sum
Build a hash map counting occurrences of each value
Use a stack to track values seen so far
items.answer: Use two pointers starting at each end, moving inward based on the current sum
items.explanation_md: A sorted array plus a pair-sum target is the classic two-pointers trigger — no heap or top-k structure is involved.

items.prompt_md: Reverse the order of nodes in a singly linked list, in place.
options: Push all node values onto a heap and pop them back in reverse order
Walk the list once, iteratively reversing each node's `next` pointer
Build a hash map from node value to node
Use two pointers converging from both ends of an array
items.answer: Walk the list once, iteratively reversing each node's `next` pointer
items.explanation_md: In-place pointer rewiring is the linked-list pattern; there's no ranking or best-k selection here, so a heap adds nothing.
